item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: This image could not be sourced, so there is no indication that is has been released under CC 3.0. As such, it is assumed to be non-compliant with CC 3.0 and has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-2140, to be exact. That one is more explicit, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Am I crazy or is there another scp that does something very similar? A symbol or something.

Either way +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd like to point out, nearly four years after the fact, how brilliant these two above comments are.

Also, unfortunately this is a thing which happened, somewhat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "In other news, the trial of the infamous Baby Bomber came to a close when the judge threw out the case, stating that it was nonsensical that 2 year old Mindy Buttons was the mastermind behind the horrific car bombing last year. Miss Buttons has been remanded into her parents' custody and will reportedly be in 'time out' for several months."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, there's an (evil) way of trying to determine if it changes the timeline which might work: get a baby who can crawl, put the baby down near the SCP, and get the baby to touch it. If the baby shows distress, but there's nothing strange about the baby's history, then it implants memories. On the other hand, if the baby did commit a violent crime, and records show that the Foundation decided to kidnap the only baby in the world who ever committed a violent crime just to see what would happen when s/he touched the SCP, then the researchers should be able to recognize the absurdity of that and concluded that the timeline must have been altered.

Of course, the thing might just not work on babies, in which case the test would fail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The last paragraph of the description really hooked me, and that fridge horror discussion up there helped too. This is awfully clever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I never saw the earlier, aparantly problematic versions, but this version is the best example of the "dawning realization" mechanic I have yet to read. The transistion from "alters holders memories" to "alters everything except the holders memories" is just perfectly executed. And if the reader is still confused, the last test note is the perfectly applied blunt instrument to drive the point home. As a cherry, the Foundation just doesn't get what's going on here, and that is entirely appropriate, because all empirical evidence points to the wrong conclusion. It's jujitsu of the mind, turning the Foundations scientific, objectivist organizational mind-set into a crippling weakness.

I am so pleased that this dodged deletion, not once but twice. Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted based on this fridge horror. (Though I wouldn't mind if another hint were added to the article, like all tests being indefinitely suspended.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoting for idea. An idea I've had before, but I'm glad to see it done better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You've got a point. I tweaked it a bit, accordingly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is very well written, but I have a hard time believing that the foundation would overlook / patently ignore the possibility of reality-affecting properties in an scp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just got hit with an absolutely delicious bit of fridge logic: the stick is not, in any way, shape or form, retroactively changing our timeline.

There is another timeline, however, where long-trusted and revered Foundation agents are suddenly confused, and acting in ways and with recollections more appropriate for D-class operatives…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, I like it just as much as before and I'm retaining my +1 vote. The multiple notes about confusion over why they're letting D-Class handle dangerous equipment helps to avoid confusion over the effects of the stick. If you really want to drive it home, you could always have a note by someone complaining that obviously, the results are because of incompetent handling from only using D-Class personnel (although fridge logic now points out to me that since only the D-Class event differs, these people would still register as "award winning physicist with multiple patents who did hideous vivisections in his basement for which he was finally caught".

Hmm… you know, it strikes me that this item is oddly Foundation-specific. Unless the stick does something to retroactively make people appropriate anathema for whatever group they are with? In which case you might get a bit more in terms of clues to the true nature by captured records from the UIU, local community, etc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Completely rewritten, with LooksLikeSnow's blessing. What do you guys think?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's actually the part that kept me from down-voting. Wasn't enough on its own to +1 though.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Oh, if something's actually happening, sure, we can keep it a bit longer.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Voting against, with trust in Photo. I like this article, so I'd like to see it rewritten. Please go quickly, Photo.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Requesting a hold on deletion. LooksLikeSnow is working on rewriting this, and I'm holding him up by not having had time to proofread. Could we have a few more days, please?

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Seconding.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Not sure if this managed to get above -5 since the first call for deletion, so I'll start anew: suggesting deletion at -5.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They did, but the agents retroactively became criminal D-Class, so they've technically never tested it on an agent.

Actually, there's some fridge horror in there:
"Hey, we've tested this thing on ten D-Class. What if we tried it on an agent or something?"
*test happens*
"Hey, we've tested this thing on eleven D-Class. What if we tried it on an agent or something?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This willow stick of denial doesn't really work for me. Got one question; why didn't the foundation test it using people without violent crimes record? Just to have a control test or something

item.title: Re: Replied to staff post :(
item.content:encoded: As much as anything makes sense, given that most SCPs work by a nonlogic of their own, it's dirty and undistinguished, it blends in. It's purposefully unremarkable in appearance to act better as a snare.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Thirding. It's not grabbing me, love the picture though.

item.title: Re: Replied to staff post :(
item.content:encoded: Why a stick? Explain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's an interesting idea, but it feels like you could do a lot more with it.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Seconding.

item.title: (not staff post any more)
item.content:encoded: You've done a good job of rewriting this, with the exception of the part where I still don't like it.

item.title: Replied to staff post :(
item.content:encoded: Sorry! Didn't know that was a faux pas, I'll avoid doing so in future. :)

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
For future reference, please don't post in anything called "Staff Post" unless you are Senior Staff. Thank you.

I vote against deletion. I really do like this one, and I'd be happy to help rewrite it. PM me, LooksLikeSnow, and we'll work on it.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Any further feedback on how to improve this article would be very welcome! I've had a stab at rewriting it top to bottom, not sure where to direct future edits.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
I'm opening a deletion vote on an article that I kind of like. The author is encouraged to work towards improving it, so this vote can be cancelled.

Calling for deletion at -5.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is messy, but is a nice idea. Neutral for now, but this is definitely salvageable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it. It can't be objectively shown to have effects, and I was actually considering a downvote up until the second test. Now I think this is fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Please redirect the following comment to SCP-073. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, define "responsible".

What if we have a D-class in a vegetative state, and we manipulate his hands so that he's the one picking up the stick?

What if your shadow falls on the stick? You're lowering its temperature.

What if you shine a flashlight on it?

What if you hold up a lens between it and sunlight, so that focused sunlight burns a line across the stick's bark?

If A picks up the stick and throws it at B, and it bounces off B, do they both retroactively become necrophiliac serial killers?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was the idea I was hunting, though I started to get tangled in the metaphysics.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: you've got a great idea here, i LOVE it. work with someone to get ideas for things to add or change, because it could definitely be improved as an article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What about robots? Is this like Cain, where it's the responsible one who gets the effect?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This could be changed. I was looking for a way of expressing that tools and gloves, or even throwing objects, doesn't help.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely love the idea of an SCP the Foundation cannot empirically prove (I tried something like that once and utterly failed to pull it off), but the effect is ruined a bit by having it teleport once it is fully contained. It makes it obviously anomalous.

If the effect is supposed to be retroactive, it would make more sense for a D-Class who was handling the item claim to be an Agent. They can't prove that the item isn't anomalous, so they aren't entirely certain if the D-Class is trying to fool them or if he really was an agent until a moment ago.

It feels like this is just a hairs breadth away from being an amazing SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Upon making contact, direct or indirect"

I'm standing on a concrete floor. The branch is on the concrete floor also.

I'm in one end of a swimming pool. The branch is dropped into the other end.

This thing seems like it would be impossible for the Foundation to even manipulate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Funny — that's what I like about it. The sentence structure makes me wince a bit, but the concept is temporally horrifying. This can earn my upvote with a bit of a grammar check.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's something wrong with this one, and I'm not quite sure if I can verbalize it.

It's converting highly-trained Foundation agents into D-class personnel with histories of having committed violent atrocities.

Retroactively.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this more than I feel that I should. Lucky you :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Meh. The idea seems nice, but the sentence structure is a little choppy and makes it a little hard to read. I'd like to see this expanded, because it strikes me as something with potential. No vote for now. Is this your first SCP?
